content_markdown:
The graph shows the variation with wavelength of intensity of radiation emitted by two bodies X and Y. X and Y have the same surface area.

How do the temperature and the emissivity of X compare with the temperature and the emissivity of Y?

diagram_metadata.description: A graph of intensity versus wavelength lambda showing two blackbody radiation curves. Curve X has a higher peak intensity than curve Y, but both curves peak at the same wavelength.

vibe_explanation: According to Wien's displacement law, the wavelength at which the intensity is maximum is inversely proportional to the absolute temperature. Since both curves X and Y peak at the same wavelength, their temperatures must be equal. However, the Stefan-Boltzmann law states that power (and thus intensity) depends on both temperature and emissivity. Since X has a higher intensity than Y at the same temperature and surface area, X must have a higher emissivity than Y, meaning their emissivities are different.
